title: Human Development Index groups
subtitle: The Human Development Index (HDI) is a summary measure of key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, a good education, and a decent standard of living. The groups below define different levels of human development depending on the HDI values.
caption:
Human Development Index groups, 2023

The Human Development Index (HDI) is a summary measure of key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, a good education, and a decent standard of living. The groups below define different levels of human development depending on the HDI values.

This choropleth map displays the global distribution of countries categorized into five distinct groups based on their Human Development Index scores for 2023. The legend defines the color scale, ranging from red for "Low" development, yellow for "Medium", light blue for "High", and dark blue for "Very high", with grey hatching indicating "No data". The highest tier, "Very high", covers most of North America, including the United States and Canada, as well as the majority of Western and Northern Europe, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, and South Korea. The "High" category encompasses much of South America, including Brazil and Argentina, alongside Russia, China, and nations in the Middle East such as Saudi Arabia. Countries in the "Medium" band are concentrated across South and Southeast Asia, including India and Indonesia, as well as parts of North Africa and Central America. The "Low" category appears predominantly across sub-Saharan Africa, covering a wide swath of the continent from the west coast through the center to the east, including nations like Nigeria, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Ethiopia. Greenland is marked with grey hatching to show that no data is available. The visual pattern shows a clear geographic stratification, with the darkest blue shades dominating the northern hemisphere's wealthy nations and lighter shades or red tones appearing more frequently in the southern hemisphere and equatorial regions.
